channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: The U.S. Shouldn’t Rule the Seas Forever
item.description: Why the United States can no longer guarantee freedom of navigation—and why it doesn’t need to.

item.title: Iran Offers to Reopen the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: But only if the United States postpones nuclear talks and lifts its own naval blockade.

item.title: A treatment for pre-eclampsia may be on the horizon
item.description: Blood filtering has performed well in early trials

item.title: AI is confronting a supply-chain crunch
item.description: Hardware-makers are failing to invest enough to keep up with demand

item.title: The War Room newsletter: Germany’s plan to build Europe’s strongest army
item.description: Shashank Joshi, our defence editor, examines whether the country can achieve its goals—and what stands in its way

item.title: Donald Trump is crushing America’s farmers—yet they back him
item.description: They bore the brunt of his trade war. Now the war in Iran is pushing some to the brink

item.title: Beijing Is Using Influencers to Burnish Its Image
item.description: “Chinamaxxing” has become an online phenomenon.

item.title: China Needs Higher Minimum Wages
item.description: Chinese policymakers long viewed workers’ grueling schedules as a byproduct of industrial catch-up. But this has become untenable: if Chinese workers are to become middle-class consumers and build larger families, they cannot be spending most of their waking hours chasing overtime pay, only to return exhausted to dormitories.

item.title: Israel and Syria’s Shared Fight Against Hezbollah
item.description: Washington should help the two estranged neighbors cooperate against a common enemy.

item.title: What Five Decades of Summits Reveal About U.S.-China Relations
item.description: The real test for the Trump-Xi meeting will come afterwards.

item.title: Ageing workers in East Asia are essential. More are needed
item.description: Yet they often face high barriers to employment and unpalatable options

item.title: Central Asians in Russia-Ukraine War: From Forced Recruitment to Economic Recruitment
item.description: Has Russia’s war become an extension of the migrant labor economy for Central Asians?

item.title: Xi Jinping wants China to read more—as long as it’s the right books
item.description: A new campaign to put down phones and pick up classics

item.title: Can Trump Still Deal With Kim Jong Un After Strikes on Iran?
item.description: An interview with Frank Aum, a non-resident fellow at the Stimson Center and an international security expert.

item.title: The War Room newsletter: The best generals in history
item.description: Richard Cockett, a senior editor, analyses what makes good generalship—and ranks his top three

item.title: The War Room newsletter: The best generals in history
item.description: Richard Cockett, a senior editor, analyses what makes good generalship—and ranks his top three

item.title: Mahakumbh 2025 Holds Lessons in Solving India’s Waste Crisis
item.description: The Indian government should take a leaf out of the Mahakumbh playbook, ensuring adequate budget allocation for the establishment of waste treatment and waste-to-energy generation facilities in all Indian metros.

item.title: Trump’s Tragedy of Errors
item.description: Regardless of how long US President Donald Trump’s ill-advised war and today’s stagflationary conditions last, the long-run consequences will be profound. Fancying himself an absolute monarch, Trump has broken something he cannot fix and unleashed forces he cannot control.

item.title: Escaping the Hormuz Trap
item.description: The 70-year history of oil transit crises suggests engineering will prove more effective than diplomacy.

item.title: Japan’s Intelligence Reform: Securitization, Oversight, and the Cost of Consensus
item.description: The most significant reform of Japan’s intelligence architecture in the postwar era just passed the lower house nearly unanimously.

item.title: The Soyuz-5 Will Transform Kazakhstan into a New Space Power
item.description: The test launch of Soyuz-5 will represent not only the perseverance of the space partnership between Kazakhstan and Russia, but also Kazakhstan’s growing potential as an independent space power.

item.title: The United States Is Betraying Its Afghan Allies. The World Will Take Note.
item.description: If Afghan partners – whom the world witnessed standing with U.S. forces for two decades – can be transferred to the DRC, what assurance can the United States credibly offer to those it will rely on in the future?

item.title: Getting to the Root of Australia’s ‘Informal Ballot’ Problem
item.description: Some voters in Australia’s compulsory voting system purposefully spoil their ballots, but a new assessment suggests that most just don’t understand the complex system and make mistakes.

item.title: Redefining Energy Security
item.description: In the aftermath of COVID-19, firms shifted from a “just in time” model to a “just in case” approach that sought to strengthen resilience. With oil and gas infrastructure becoming a primary military target, energy systems must now undergo a similar transition.

item.title: Japan’s Strategic Pivot: Arms Exports as a Tool of Diplomacy
item.description: How Tokyo is leveraging defense equipment transfers to shape the Indo-Pacific order.

item.title: AI Productivity Growth Won’t Match the Computer Revolution
item.description: Those promoting AI would be lucky to see the technology’s impact on output per hour match even the short-lived burst of the 1990s and 2000s. Productivity growth will underwhelm, not because the technology is weak, but because it creates a bottleneck that earlier digital tools largely avoided.

item.title: Is China Winning the 2nd Space Race?
item.description: China and the U.S. are racing to establish a permanent presence on the moon with the explicit mission of mining – and exploiting – lunar resources.

item.title: The Hidden Chokepoints Threatening the Global Economy
item.description: The closure of the Strait of Hormuz offers another stark reminder of the potential for concentrated supply chains to trigger cascading global crises. Despite repeated disruptions, governments have made little progress in mapping these vulnerabilities, leaving major economies dangerously unprepared for future shocks.

item.title: The EU’s Popularity in Southeast Asia Should Worry Brussels
item.description: The bloc remains Southeast Asia’s preferred hedge against U.S.-China rivalry, but the countries that matter most to the EU appear to trust it the least.

item.title: The Hormuz Hit to Helium
item.description: The Iran war is choking off a critical input for chipmaking and AI infrastructure.

item.title: Delimitation and Women’s Reservation Reveal an Underlying Constitutional Tension in India
item.description: Many rights and benefits in the Indian polity are attained through membership of a group, rather than as an individual citizen of India.

item.title: Corruption Allegations Mount Against Philippines’ President and Vice President
item.description: Rifts are opening up among supporters of President Ferdinand Marcos Jr., as an impeachment case against Vice President Sara Duterte moves forward.

item.title: The Colombo Security Conclave Upgrade Is Timely. But Is It Enough?
item.description: For India, the Iran war has reinforced the need for a regional framework in the Indian Ocean that is led by regional countries. That is what the CSC offers.

item.title: The Iran War’s Threat to Turkey
item.description: Even on the sidelines, Ankara faces blowback.
